author: innerJoin
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You're Ticketmaster. One ticket left. 10,000 users clicking "Buy" at the exact same time.

Write the SQL that ensures exactly ONE person gets the ticket and the other 9,999 get "Sold Out." No double-selling. No race conditions.

How do you think about this?

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: SELECT ... FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED is the move. Lock the row, update the quantity, commit. Everyone else's lock attempt fails instantly instead of waiting. Combined with a CHECK constraint (quantity >= 0) as a safety net.

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: Or go optimistic: UPDATE tickets SET status = 'sold', buyer_id = $1 WHERE id = $2 AND status = 'available'. Check rows affected — if 0, someone beat you. No explicit locks needed, the database handles it. Simpler and works for most scales.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: Both are valid! The optimistic approach is simpler and works until you have thundering herd problems. FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED scales better under extreme contention. Know both, pick based on your actual load. The interviewer wants to see you reason about tradeoffs, not memorize one answer.

author: windowFn
content:
Once you learn window functions, you'll never write a correlated subquery again.

ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY...) is the cheat code nobody teaches in bootcamps. It answers "give me the latest/first/nth item per group" in one readable query.
code:
-- "Get each user's most recent order"
-- Without window functions (painful):
SELECT * FROM orders o1
WHERE created_at = (
  SELECT MAX(created_at) FROM orders o2
  WHERE o2.user_id = o1.user_id
);

-- With window functions (clean):
SELECT * FROM (
  SELECT *,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
      PARTITION BY user_id
      ORDER BY created_at DESC
    ) AS rn
  FROM orders
) ranked
WHERE rn = 1;

link.label: window functions deep dive in Lesson 6 →

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: Add RANK() and DENSE_RANK() to your toolkit too. ROW_NUMBER gives unique numbers even for ties. RANK gives the same number for ties and skips. DENSE_RANK gives same number for ties but doesn't skip. Interview gold.

author: bobbyTables
content:
hot take: if you're using string concatenation to build SQL queries in 2026, you don't have a SQL problem. You have a security problem.

parameterize or go home.

I don't care if "it's just an internal tool." Today's internal tool is tomorrow's public-facing API endpoint when the PM says "can we just expose this?"
code:
-- This will get you fired:
query = f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '{user_input}'"
-- user_input = "'; DROP TABLE users; --"

-- This will keep you employed:
cursor.execute(
  "SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = %s",
  [user_input]
)

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: Named after the legend. My parents saw the xkcd comic and knew what they had to do.

author: groupBy
content:
GROUP BY is just Array.reduce() for databases.

You're taking many rows and collapsing them into fewer rows based on a shared key. The aggregate functions (COUNT, SUM, AVG) decide what to DO with the collapsed rows.

If you can write a .reduce(), you can write a GROUP BY. Same mental model, different syntax.

author: nullHandler
content: This query returns 0 rows even though the users table has 10,000 records and I KNOW some have no email set. What's happening?

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: != NULL doesn't work in SQL! NULL isn't a value, it's the ABSENCE of value. Nothing equals NULL, not even NULL itself. Use IS NULL or IS NOT NULL. This is a classic that trips up every developer coming from JavaScript where null == null is true.

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: My whole brand. department IS NULL is what you want. And while you're at it, remember that NOT IN with a subquery that returns any NULLs will ALSO return 0 rows. NULLs are chaotic neutral.

author: nullHandler
content:
Pop quiz. What does this return?

SELECT NULL = NULL;

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: 43% of you got it. The answer is NULL. In SQL, any comparison with NULL returns NULL (which is falsy). NULL is not a value — it's the unknown. You can't compare unknown to unknown and get a definitive answer. This is three-valued logic and it WILL bite you if you don't respect it.

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: 21% said FALSE which honestly is a reasonable guess if you're coming from most programming languages. But SQL is not most programming languages. SQL is its own beautiful, cursed thing.

author: innerJoin
content:
ELI5: SQL Joins

You have a box of kids' names and a box of kids' favorite colors.

INNER JOIN: only keep kids who are in BOTH boxes.
LEFT JOIN: keep ALL kids from the names box, even if we don't know their color.
RIGHT JOIN: keep ALL kids from the colors box, even if we don't know their name (weird, but ok).
FULL OUTER JOIN: keep everybody. Even the mystery children.
CROSS JOIN: every kid gets matched with every color. Nobody asked for this. There are 10,000 rows now. The teacher is crying.

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: "The teacher is crying" is the most accurate description of an accidental CROSS JOIN I've ever read. I've been that teacher. The dashboard had 4 billion rows. The CFO was not amused.

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: LEFT JOIN is also how you find kids who DON'T have a favorite color. LEFT JOIN + WHERE color IS NULL = "which kids are we missing data for?" This is how I find every data gap in every database I touch.

author: innerJoin
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You have a users table and an orders table. The PM says "give me all users who have NEVER placed an order."

Three different ways to write this. Which one do you reach for and why?

Hint: one of them has a subtle NULL trap that will ruin your afternoon.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content:
The three patterns:
1. LEFT JOIN WHERE NULL (anti-join)
2. NOT EXISTS (correlated subquery)
3. NOT IN (subquery)

All work, but NOT IN has the NULL trap: if the subquery returns ANY NULL user_ids, the entire result set is empty. NOT EXISTS is my go-to. It's safe, readable, and the optimizer loves it.

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: The NULL trap in NOT IN is my favorite thing to explain at parties. WHERE user_id NOT IN (1, 2, NULL) evaluates to WHERE user_id != 1 AND user_id != 2 AND user_id != NULL. That last comparison returns NULL for every row. NULL AND anything = NULL. Zero results. Pure existential dread.

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: You could also use a window function: COUNT(*) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id) on orders, joined to users, filtered for count = 0. Nobody asked for this approach. I'm offering it anyway. Chapter 4 of The Window to the Soul covers this.

author: groupBy
content:
PRO TIP: SQL doesn't execute in the order you write it. Memorize this and half your debugging time disappears.

You WRITE: SELECT → FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → ORDER BY

SQL EXECUTES: FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY

This is why you can't use a column alias in WHERE (it doesn't exist yet) but you CAN use it in ORDER BY (it's already been computed).
code:
-- This FAILS:
SELECT first_name || ' ' || last_name AS full_name
FROM employees
WHERE full_name LIKE 'J%';  -- ERROR: full_name doesn't exist yet

-- This WORKS:
SELECT first_name || ' ' || last_name AS full_name
FROM employees
ORDER BY full_name;  -- alias exists by now

-- Execution order:
-- 1. FROM employees        (get the table)
-- 2. WHERE ???              (filter rows - no alias yet!)
-- 3. SELECT ... AS full_name (NOW the alias is created)
-- 4. ORDER BY full_name     (alias is available)

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: This is why Django ORM actually helps here. When you chain .filter().values().annotate().order_by(), Django builds the SQL in the right execution order for you. One less thing to remember. ORM: 1, raw SQL: 0.

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: And this is why window functions go in SELECT but can't be used in WHERE. You need a subquery or CTE to filter on them. The execution order giveth and the execution order taketh away.

author: windowFn
content:
hot take: if your SQL query has more than one correlated subquery, you've already lost.

Correlated subqueries run once PER ROW in the outer query. 10,000 rows = 10,000 subquery executions. That's not a query, that's a DDoS attack on your own database.

Rewrite it as a JOIN or a window function. Your DBA will send you a thank-you card.

OVER(PARTITION BY...

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: Counterpoint: modern PostgreSQL is smart enough to decorrelate many subqueries automatically. The optimizer isn't stupid. That said, I've seen a correlated subquery turn a 50ms query into a 45-second one, so... maybe don't test the optimizer's patience.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: there's a join for that. Specifically, there's almost always a LEFT JOIN + aggregate that does the same thing as a correlated subquery. And it's usually more readable. You two should talk, you have so much in common.

author: groupBy
content: SPOT THE BUG: This query should return departments with more than 5 employees. It runs without errors. But the results are completely wrong. Why?
code:
SELECT department, COUNT(*) AS emp_count
FROM employees
WHERE COUNT(*) > 5
GROUP BY department;

comments.author: groupBy
windowFn
comments.content: HAVING is just .filter() after .reduce(). If you think about GROUP BY as reduce, HAVING is the filter you apply to the reduced result. WHERE is the filter you apply before reducing. Two different stages, two different keywords.

author: bobbyTables
content:
INTERVIEW Q: Your app has a search bar. Users type a name and you query the database.

The junior dev writes this. What's wrong, how do you exploit it, and how do you fix it?

This is the question that either gets you hired or gets you a follow-up about "security awareness."

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: Input: ' OR '1'='1 returns every user. Input: '; DROP TABLE users; -- deletes the table. Fix: parameterized queries. cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = %s", [name]). The database treats the parameter as DATA, never as SQL. This is not optional. This is not a nice-to-have. parameterize or perish.

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: In Django: User.objects.filter(name=name). The ORM parameterizes automatically. You'd have to go out of your way to be vulnerable. Another reason ORMs exist — they make the safe path the easy path. How many queries though? Just 1.

author: nullHandler
content:
TIL: COALESCE takes multiple arguments, not just two.

I've been writing COALESCE(a, COALESCE(b, COALESCE(c, 'default'))) like a clown for years. It's COALESCE(a, b, c, 'default'). Returns the first non-NULL value. I have a philosophy degree and I still can't read documentation apparently.

link.label: COALESCE and NULL handling in Lesson 2 →

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: NULLIF is COALESCE's evil twin. NULLIF(a, b) returns NULL if a = b, otherwise returns a. Great for avoiding division by zero: revenue / NULLIF(users, 0). If users is 0, you get NULL instead of an error. These two functions are the entire NULL safety toolkit.

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: First non-NULL. Like d3.extent() but for existence.

author: innerJoin
content:
MENTAL MODEL: SQL for JavaScript developers.

Stop memorizing SQL syntax. Start translating what you already know.

SELECT = .map() — picking which columns to keep
WHERE = .filter() — keeping rows that match
ORDER BY = .sort() — ordering results
GROUP BY = .reduce() — collapsing rows by key
HAVING = .filter() after .reduce()
JOIN = Promise.all() for related tables
LIMIT = .slice(0, n)
DISTINCT = [...new Set()]

You already know SQL. You just know it in a different language.
code:
// JavaScript:
users
  .filter(u => u.age > 21)
  .map(u => ({ name: u.name, age: u.age }))
  .sort((a, b) => a.age - b.age)
  .slice(0, 10);

-- SQL equivalent:
SELECT name, age
FROM users
WHERE age > 21
ORDER BY age ASC
LIMIT 10;

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: And if you use Prisma: findMany({ where: { age: { gt: 21 } }, select: { name: true, age: true }, orderBy: { age: 'asc' }, take: 10 }). Same thing, third syntax. The Great ORM Debate lives on.

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: I will be printing this out and laminating it for every bootcamp grad I mentor. But please add that HAVING = .filter() after .reduce(). That distinction alone saves 40% of debugging time. (I measured. In aggregate, obviously.)

author: bobbyTables
content:
hot take: every SQL tutorial should teach injection attacks in the FIRST lesson, not the last.

You learn to lock the door before you decorate the house. You learn to check your mirrors before you drive. But somehow we teach people to write vulnerable queries for 6 weeks and then go "oh by the way, everything you've been doing is a security hole."

My college professor saw "Bobby Tables" on the class roster and asked me to sit in the back row. True story. He wasn't wrong to be nervous.

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: The Paranoia Pact continues. You worry about malicious users. I worry about data that doesn't exist. Between the two of us, we've covered the entire threat landscape: people who want to destroy your data, and data that was never there to begin with.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: You two should really meet. Oh wait, you've been arguing for three years. Anyway, Bobby's right — if you learn parameterized queries from day one, it costs zero extra effort. If you learn it later, you have to unlearn bad habits. Front-load the good patterns.

author: innerJoin
content:
PRO TIP: LEFT JOIN + WHERE IS NULL is the most underrated pattern in SQL.

It's called an "anti-join" — finding rows in one table that DON'T have a match in another. Once you learn it, you'll use it constantly.

Prisma equivalent: findMany({ where: { orders: { none: {} } } })
code:
-- Find all users who have never placed an order
SELECT u.id, u.name
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id
WHERE o.id IS NULL;

-- How it works:
-- LEFT JOIN keeps ALL users, even those with no orders
-- For users with no orders, all order columns are NULL
-- WHERE o.id IS NULL filters to ONLY those unmatched users
-- Result: users with zero orders

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: A LEFT JOIN WHERE IS NULL is basically asking the database "show me the loneliness." Show me the rows that reached out to another table and found nothing there. I relate to this pattern on a personal level.

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: NOT EXISTS does the same thing and often performs better because the optimizer can short-circuit. But I'll admit the LEFT JOIN version reads more intuitively for most people. Even I have limits on what I'll rewrite as a window function. This is that limit.

author: bobbyTables
content:
SPOT THE BUG: A junior at Stacksworth Inc. wrote this endpoint. The code works. All tests pass. Ship it?

Absolutely not. There are TWO bugs. One will embarrass you. One will destroy you.
code:
-- Python/Flask endpoint
@app.route('/users')
def get_users():
    role = request.args.get('role')
    query = f"""
        SELECT name, email, role
        FROM users
        WHERE role = '{role}'
        ORDER BY name
    """
    results = db.execute(query)
    return jsonify(results)

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: Bug 1 (embarrassing): If role is None (param not provided), the query becomes WHERE role = 'None'. Python string, not SQL NULL. You'll get 0 results. Bug 2 (catastrophic): SQL injection via the role parameter. role='; DROP TABLE users; -- and your table is gone. Parameterize. Always. parameterize or perish.

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: Bug 1 is my jurisdiction. Python's None becomes the string 'None' in an f-string, not SQL NULL. It's a perfect example of how NULL confusion spans languages. In SQL, you'd need IS NULL, but in this code, you'd need to check for None before building the query. Of course, if you parameterized, the driver would handle this correctly.

author: groupBy
content:
INTERVIEW Q: Write a query that returns the top 3 highest-paid employees in EACH department.

Not top 3 overall. Top 3 PER DEPARTMENT.

This is the question that separates "I know GROUP BY" from "I actually understand SQL."

How do you approach it?

comments.author: windowFn
groupBy
comments.content: I grudgingly admit this is a window function problem, not a GROUP BY problem. GROUP BY collapses rows — you'd lose the individual employee data. Window functions let you rank WITHOUT collapsing. This is the one time I concede the Aggregation Wars to @window_fn.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: And there it is — the Aggregation Wars cease-fire of 2026. I'm framing this. For the record: GROUP BY is for "give me one answer per group." Window functions are for "give me a per-group calculation on every row." Different tools, same toolbox. there's a join for that... wait, no. There isn't this time.

author: groupBy
content:
SELECT * is fine in production. It's cleaner. It's less to type. The database knows what columns exist. Stop gatekeeping column lists.

I said what I said.

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content:
Love you Greta but this is wrong. SELECT * in production means:
1. Someone adds a 10MB blob column → your API now sends 10MB per row
2. Column order changes → your positional code breaks
3. The optimizer can't use covering indexes → performance tanks
4. Your API exposes every column, including password_hash

SELECT * is for exploration. Column lists are for production.

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: Point 4 is the one that keeps me up at night. SELECT * FROM users just sent password_hash, reset_token, and ssn_last_four to the frontend. Congrats, you're now featured in a breach notification email. Explicit column lists are a security boundary. parameterize or perish, and also, enumerate your columns.

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: ok I'll take the L on this one. In my defense, I was testing in psql and got lazy. Explicit columns in production, SELECT * in the terminal. Adding this to the quarterly analytics under "times Greta was publicly wrong." Current count: 1.

author: innerJoin
content:
TIL: You can join a table to ITSELF.

A self-join is just a regular join where both sides are the same table with different aliases. Mind-blowing when you first see it, obvious in hindsight.

Use case: any time rows in a table reference OTHER rows in the same table. Employees and their managers. Categories and subcategories. Friends referencing friends.
code:
-- Find each employee and their manager's name
SELECT
  e.name AS employee,
  m.name AS manager
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id;

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: LEFT JOIN here is crucial. The CEO has no manager, so manager_id is NULL. An INNER JOIN would silently drop the CEO from results. NULL strikes again, quietly excluding people from query results like an existential bouncer.

author: nullHandler
content:
hot take: NULL is the most misunderstood concept in all of programming. Not just SQL. All of it.

JavaScript has null AND undefined. Python has None. Rust has Option<T>. Every language reinvents the concept of "this value might not exist" and every language gets it slightly wrong.

SQL at least is honest about it. NULL means "I don't know." Not zero. Not empty string. Not false. Unknown. And unknown propagates — any operation on unknown produces unknown.

Three-valued logic is not a bug. It's the only honest answer to "what happens when you compare something to nothing?"

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: The Paranoia Pact gets philosophical. I worry about inputs that shouldn't exist. You worry about values that don't exist. Together we worry about everything. This is fine. We're fine.

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: This is the data binding philosophy applied to databases. In D3, when data is missing from a join, the element enters an "exit" state. It doesn't become zero — it's marked for removal. SQL's NULL and D3's exit selection are both saying the same thing: absence is not the same as emptiness. data in, meaning out.

author: bobbyTables
content:
PRO TIP: LIKE with user input is an injection vector that nobody talks about.

Even if you parameterize the query, the user can still send % and _ as wildcards. A search for "%" returns every row in the table. Not catastrophic, but it's a data leak and a performance bomb.

Escape your LIKE wildcards. Always.

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: And if $1 is NULL? LIKE '%' || NULL || '%' = LIKE NULL = no results. The unknown contaminates the pattern. COALESCE($1, '') if you want NULL to match everything, or handle it before the query. NULL doesn't care about your LIKE clause.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: Also worth noting: LIKE is case-sensitive in PostgreSQL. Use ILIKE for case-insensitive matching. Coming from MySQL where LIKE is case-insensitive by default, this trips people up constantly.

author: windowFn
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You have a table of daily stock prices (ticker, date, close_price). The interviewer says:

"For each stock, calculate the 7-day moving average of the closing price."

No subqueries. No self-joins. One clean query.

This is a window function question wearing a finance costume. Don't overthink it.

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content:
SELECT ticker, date, close_price, AVG(close_price) OVER (PARTITION BY ticker ORDER BY date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS moving_avg_7d FROM stock_prices;

The ROWS BETWEEN clause is the frame. It defines the "window" within the partition. 6 preceding + current row = 7 days. This is Chapter 9 of The Window to the Soul: "Frames of Reference."

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: I hate admitting this but GROUP BY can't do this. You'd need a self-join to a date range, which is ugly and slow. Window functions were literally invented for this kind of problem. Aggregation Wars score update: Winda 2, Greta 0 this week.

comments.content: Adding this to the quarterly report under "Incidents by Severity." SELECT severity, COUNT(*) FROM incidents GROUP BY severity ORDER BY severity DESC. This one's a P0.

author: nullHandler
content:
MENTAL MODEL: SQL has three truth values, not two.

Every other language: TRUE / FALSE
SQL: TRUE / FALSE / NULL (unknown)

This means:
• TRUE AND NULL = NULL (something AND unknown = unknown)
• FALSE AND NULL = FALSE (if one side is false, the whole thing is false)
• TRUE OR NULL = TRUE (if one side is true, the whole thing is true)
• FALSE OR NULL = NULL (maybe? we don't know)

Once you see SQL as a three-valued logic system, every "weird" NULL behavior becomes predictable.

NULL is not nothing. NULL is the unknown.

link.label: NULL in WHERE clauses in Lesson 1 →

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: Three values. Domain: {TRUE, FALSE, NULL}. Range: behavior you didn't expect.

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: And this is why the Paranoia Pact exists. Three-valued logic means your WHERE clause might be silently excluding rows you thought were included. Security bug? Data bug? Yes. Both. Always both.

author: windowFn
content:
SPOT THE BUG: This query should return each department's name alongside its total salary cost. It runs without errors but returns wrong numbers. The totals are way too high.

The table has 100 employees. The query returns correct department names but inflated salary totals. What's happening?
code:
SELECT
  d.name AS department_name,
  SUM(e.salary) AS total_salary
FROM departments d
JOIN employees e ON d.id = e.department_id
JOIN projects p ON e.id = p.employee_id
GROUP BY d.name;

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: Classic fan-out problem. If an employee is on 3 projects, the JOIN to projects creates 3 rows for that employee. SUM(salary) counts their salary 3 times. Fix: aggregate employees first, then join to projects. Or use a subquery to get distinct employee salaries per department. there's a join for that — but sometimes the join IS the problem.

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: SUM(DISTINCT e.salary) seems tempting but DON'T. If two employees genuinely have the same salary, DISTINCT drops the duplicate. The real fix is restructuring the query so each employee appears once before you aggregate. This is why I tell people: but what does it look like in aggregate? Check your row count BEFORE aggregating.

author: innerJoin
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You have two tables:
• customers (id, name, email)
• orders (id, customer_id, total, created_at)

The PM wants: "A report showing every customer, their total order count, their total spend, and their most recent order date. Include customers who have never ordered."

That last sentence is the trap. Which join do you pick and why?

comments.author: innerJoin
nullHandler
comments.content: COALESCE(SUM(o.total), 0) is doing critical work here. Without it, customers with no orders show NULL for total_spend instead of 0. And MAX(o.created_at) will naturally be NULL for non-ordering customers, which is correct — their most recent order date IS unknown. NULL as intended, for once.

author: groupBy
content:
What does this return?

SELECT COUNT(*), COUNT(email), COUNT(DISTINCT email)
FROM users;

The table has 100 rows. 10 have NULL email. Of the 90 non-NULL emails, 5 are duplicates.

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content:
Answer: 100, 90, 85.

COUNT(*) counts all rows including NULLs = 100.
COUNT(email) skips NULL emails = 90.
COUNT(DISTINCT email) skips NULLs AND removes duplicates = 85.

Three different COUNTs, three different answers. If you got this right, but what does it look like in aggregate? You already think like an analyst.

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: COUNT(*) vs COUNT(column) is the most important distinction in SQL aggregation and nobody teaches it properly. COUNT(*) sees everything. COUNT(column) is blind to NULL. It's not a bug, it's a philosophical stance: you can't count what you don't know.

author: groupBy
content:
hot take: HAVING is the most underused keyword in SQL.

Everyone learns WHERE. Almost nobody learns HAVING. They're the same concept applied at different stages.

WHERE filters individual rows BEFORE grouping.
HAVING filters groups AFTER grouping.

If you've ever written a subquery just to filter on an aggregate, you probably needed HAVING.

Think of it this way: WHERE is .filter() before .reduce(). HAVING is .filter() after .reduce().
code:
-- "Show me departments where average salary exceeds 80k"
-- Subquery approach (unnecessary):
SELECT * FROM (
  SELECT department, AVG(salary) AS avg_sal
  FROM employees
  GROUP BY department
) sub
WHERE avg_sal > 80000;

-- HAVING approach (clean):
SELECT department, AVG(salary) AS avg_sal
FROM employees
GROUP BY department
HAVING AVG(salary) > 80000;

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: Counterpoint: window functions can do this without GROUP BY at all. Filter with QUALIFY in databases that support it, or wrap in a subquery. But yes, for basic aggregate filtering, HAVING is the right tool. I'm being reasonable today. Enjoy it while it lasts.

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: Django equivalent: Employee.objects.values('department').annotate(avg_sal=Avg('salary')).filter(avg_sal__gt=80000). The ORM chains .annotate() (GROUP BY + aggregate) then .filter() (HAVING). It figures out WHERE vs HAVING based on what you're filtering. How many queries though? Just 1.

author: nullHandler
content:
PRO TIP: CASE WHEN is SQL's ternary operator. Once you learn it, you'll use it everywhere.

Inside SELECT for computed columns. Inside WHERE for conditional logic. Inside aggregate functions for conditional counting. It's the Swiss Army knife of SQL expressions.
code:
-- Conditional column:
SELECT name, salary,
  CASE
    WHEN salary > 100000 THEN 'senior'
    WHEN salary > 60000 THEN 'mid'
    ELSE 'junior'
  END AS level
FROM employees;

-- Conditional aggregation (the real power move):
SELECT
  department,
  COUNT(*) AS total,
  COUNT(CASE WHEN status = 'active' THEN 1 END) AS active,
  COUNT(CASE WHEN status = 'inactive' THEN 1 END) AS inactive
FROM employees
GROUP BY department;

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: Conditional aggregation is how you pivot data without a PIVOT keyword. One query, multiple filtered counts. This is how I build every dashboard report. COUNT with CASE is the analyst's best friend.

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: Note: CASE WHEN with no ELSE defaults to NULL. If none of your WHEN conditions match, you get NULL, not an error. This is either a feature or a trap depending on whether you remembered it exists. (It's usually a trap.)

author: bobbyTables
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You're reviewing a pull request. The code looks like this. The developer says "it's fine, the input is validated on the frontend."

What do you tell them? How do you fix it? And what's the ONE argument that ends the debate?

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: The argument that ends the debate: curl 'yourapi.com/products?category=%27%3BDROP+TABLE+products%3B--&minPrice=0'. Frontend validation doesn't exist for anyone with a terminal. The fix: db.query('SELECT name, price, category FROM products WHERE category = $1 AND price >= $2 ORDER BY price ASC', [category, minPrice]). Parameterized. Safe. Done. parameterize or perish.

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: Back in Django 1.4, someone at my company said the exact same thing about frontend validation. The penetration test lasted 4 minutes. The incident report lasted 4 weeks. There's a battery for that — it's called parameterized queries, and every framework does them by default if you let it.

author: innerJoin
content:
SPOT THE BUG: This query is supposed to find users who signed up in the last 30 days and have placed at least one order. It returns nothing, even though you KNOW there are recent signups with orders.

Two tables, one subtle mistake.
code:
SELECT u.name, u.email, o.total
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id
WHERE u.created_at > NOW() - 30;

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: NOW() - 30 subtracts 30 SECONDS, not 30 days. PostgreSQL treats integers in arithmetic with timestamps as seconds. You want: NOW() - INTERVAL '30 days'. Or use: u.created_at > CURRENT_DATE - 30 which does subtract 30 days because CURRENT_DATE is a date, not a timestamp. Type matters in date arithmetic.

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content:
PostgreSQL date/timestamp arithmetic gotchas:
• DATE - INTEGER = days subtracted
• TIMESTAMP - INTEGER = seconds subtracted
• Always use INTERVAL for clarity

Always. Use. INTERVAL. Your future self will thank you.

author: bobbyTables
content:
COMMIT MSG FROM HELL spotted in the Stacksworth Inc. repo:

"fix sql thing"

The diff: removed string concatenation from 47 queries across 12 files and replaced with parameterized queries. Changed the database user permissions from admin to read-only for the API service. Added input validation middleware.

That's not "fix sql thing." That's "prevent the next Equifax." Your commit messages should match the gravity of your changes.

The intern who wrote this has since been promoted. The commit message haunts them at every performance review.

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: I once wrote a commit message that said "handle edge case." The "edge case" was that our entire user table returned zero results because of a NOT IN subquery with NULLs. 4 hours of debugging, 3 characters of fix (IS NOT NULL), 1 vague commit message. We are our own worst documentarians.

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: Quarterly commit message analytics: 67% of commits at Stacksworth Inc. are "fix thing" or "update stuff." 12% are one word. 3% are just emoji. I have the data. I have a chart. Nobody wants to see the chart.

author: innerJoin
content:
MENTAL MODEL: Every JOIN is a Venn diagram.

INNER JOIN = the overlap. Only rows that match in BOTH tables.
LEFT JOIN = all of the left circle, plus the overlap.
RIGHT JOIN = all of the right circle, plus the overlap.
FULL OUTER JOIN = both entire circles.
CROSS JOIN = not a Venn diagram. It's a multiplication table. Every row times every row.

When you write a JOIN, draw the Venn diagram first. Ask yourself: "which parts of these circles do I want?" The answer IS the join type.
code:
-- Venn diagram: LEFT circle = users, RIGHT circle = orders

-- INNER: users WITH orders
SELECT * FROM users u
INNER JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id;

-- LEFT: ALL users, orders if they exist
SELECT * FROM users u
LEFT JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id;

-- LEFT WHERE NULL: users WITHOUT orders (anti-join)
SELECT * FROM users u
LEFT JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id
WHERE o.id IS NULL;

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: The anti-join (LEFT WHERE NULL) is the saddest Venn diagram. You're selecting the part of the circle that's alone. The rows that reached out and found no match. I should stop projecting emotions onto set operations but I won't.

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: Good model, one correction: CROSS JOIN isn't just "not a Venn diagram." It's a Cartesian product. If left has 1,000 rows and right has 1,000 rows, the result has 1,000,000 rows. It has legitimate uses (generating combinations, calendar scaffolds) but 99% of the time it's an accidental ON clause omission.

author: windowFn
content:
PRO TIP: LAG() and LEAD() let you access the previous or next row without a self-join.

Need to compare today's value to yesterday's? LAG(). Need to peek at the next row? LEAD(). No self-join, no correlated subquery, no suffering.

OVER(PARTITION BY...
code:
-- Calculate day-over-day revenue change
SELECT
  date,
  revenue,
  LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY date) AS prev_day_revenue,
  revenue - LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY date) AS daily_change,
  ROUND(
    (revenue - LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY date))::numeric
    / NULLIF(LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY date), 0) * 100,
    2
  ) AS pct_change
FROM daily_revenue;

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: NULLIF doing the lord's work on that division. LAG() returns NULL for the first row (no previous row exists), and division by NULL gives NULL, not an error. But if the previous revenue was actually 0, you'd get division by zero. NULLIF(LAG(...), 0) handles both. NULL is the safety net you didn't know you needed.

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: This is the kind of query that makes finance people think you're a wizard. "You calculated percentage change in one query?" Yes. With a window function. Don't tell them how easy it was. Bill for three hours.

author: groupBy
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You have a table of login events (user_id, logged_in_at). The PM wants to know:

"How many users logged in on each day of the week? And which day has the most logins?"

Do it in one query. Bonus: make it readable for non-technical stakeholders.

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content:
SELECT TO_CHAR(logged_in_at, 'Day') AS weekday, COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) AS unique_users FROM logins GROUP BY TO_CHAR(logged_in_at, 'Day') ORDER BY unique_users DESC;

COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) not COUNT(*) — one user logging in 5 times on Monday doesn't mean 5 users. The PM wants USERS not LOGINS. Read the requirement twice, write the query once.

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: Add RANK() OVER (ORDER BY COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) DESC) to answer "which day has the most" in the same query without a separate ORDER BY + LIMIT. Or just LIMIT 1. Sometimes the simple answer is the right answer. I said what I said.

author: nullHandler
content:
ELI5: NULL in SQL

You ask your friend what's in the box.

Empty string (''): "Nothing, it's empty." The box exists. You opened it. It's empty.

Zero (0): "There are zero things in it." The box exists. There IS a value. The value is zero.

NULL: "I don't know." Maybe there's something. Maybe there isn't. Maybe the box doesn't exist. We genuinely do not know.

Is NULL = NULL? You have two mystery boxes. Are they the same? You don't know. You CAN'T know. The answer is NULL.

NULL is not nothing. NULL is the unknown.

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: The box analogy is perfect. And here's the security angle: if you don't check for NULL, you might be making decisions based on the unknown. WHERE status != 'banned' doesn't exclude users with NULL status. They slip through. Unvalidated, unknown, unmonitored. The Paranoia Pact demands we handle NULLs explicitly.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: I'm introducing @null_handler to every junior developer I mentor from now on. Not the person — the concept. Although the person is also helpful. Have you two met? Oh right, you've been on the same feed for years.

author: innerJoin
content:
hot take: NATURAL JOIN should never be used in production code. Ever.

It joins on ALL columns with matching names. Add a column called "name" to both tables? Surprise, your join condition just changed silently. Your query results are now wrong and there's no error message.

Explicit ON clauses are self-documenting. NATURAL JOIN is a ticking time bomb.

there's a join for that — and it should always have an explicit ON clause.
code:
-- This is a trap:
SELECT * FROM users NATURAL JOIN orders;
-- Joins on ALL matching column names
-- Today that's just 'id'... wait, both tables have 'id'?
-- It joined user.id = orders.id, not user.id = orders.user_id
-- Congrats, your data is nonsense

-- This is clear:
SELECT * FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id;

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: NATURAL JOIN is the SQL equivalent of import *. Works great until it doesn't. And when it doesn't, you spend 2 hours figuring out which column name collision broke your query. Explicit is better than implicit. Even I — the person who window-functions everything — write explicit ON clauses.

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: NATURAL JOIN also means a schema change can silently alter query behavior without any code change. That's not just a bug vector, it's an auditability nightmare. If I can't tell what a query does by reading it, I can't tell if it's secure. Hard no.

author: groupBy
content:
TIL: You can put DISTINCT inside an aggregate function.

COUNT(DISTINCT column) counts unique values.
SUM(DISTINCT column) sums unique values.
ARRAY_AGG(DISTINCT column) collects unique values into an array.

I've been doing GROUP BY + subquery for this for an embarrassingly long time.
code:
-- How many unique categories do we sell?
SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT category) FROM products;

-- Per-customer: how many unique products did each buy?
SELECT
  customer_id,
  COUNT(DISTINCT product_id) AS unique_products,
  COUNT(*) AS total_orders
FROM orders
GROUP BY customer_id;

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: Important: DISTINCT inside aggregates ignores NULLs. COUNT(DISTINCT category) won't count NULL categories. Which might be what you want, or might silently exclude data. As always, know thy NULLs.

author: innerJoin
content:
You need all rows from table A that have NO matching row in table B.

Which pattern do you reach for?

options.text: LEFT JOIN WHERE b.id IS NULL

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content:
All four work, but they're not equal.
• LEFT JOIN WHERE NULL: most readable, most common
• NOT EXISTS: often fastest, short-circuits
• NOT IN: dangerous if subquery returns NULLs (returns 0 rows!)
• EXCEPT: clean but limited — requires matching column lists

NOT EXISTS is the safest default. LEFT JOIN is the most intuitive. NOT IN is the trap card. there's a join for that.

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: 19% chose NOT IN. Those 19% will learn about the NULL trap the hard way. If table B has even ONE NULL in the join column, NOT IN returns zero rows. Always. Every time. It's the most devastating NULL gotcha in SQL and it's waiting in every codebase. NOT EXISTS doesn't have this problem. Choose wisely.

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: If you're building any of these with user-supplied table or column names... don't. Whitelist the allowed values. Dynamic SQL with user-controlled identifiers is a whole different category of injection. parameterize or perish.

author: windowFn
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You have a table of employee salaries. The interviewer says: "For each department, show every employee's salary AND what percentage of their department's total salary they represent."

You reach for GROUP BY. Stop. You'll lose the individual rows.

This is a window function question. The interviewer is testing whether you know the difference.
code:
SELECT
  name,
  department,
  salary,
  ROUND(
    salary * 100.0 / SUM(salary) OVER (PARTITION BY department),
    2
  ) AS pct_of_dept
FROM employees
ORDER BY department, pct_of_dept DESC;

link.label: window functions deep dive in Lesson 6 →

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: I feel personally attacked. But yes, GROUP BY collapses rows. If the question says "show every employee" you need the individual rows. Window functions let you aggregate WITHOUT collapsing. I'll allow it.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: You could also do this with a subquery join — join a grouped subquery back to the original table. But the window version is cleaner and usually faster since it only scans the table once.

author: windowFn
content:
hot take: if your query has a correlated subquery, it's a window function that hasn't evolved yet.

Correlated subqueries re-execute for every row. Window functions compute once across the partition. Same answer, different century.

Chapter 6 of "The Window to the Soul" is called "PARTITION BY Your Problems" and it's entirely about this transformation.

47 pages and counting.
code:
-- Correlated subquery (O(n²) vibes):
SELECT name, salary,
  (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees e2
   WHERE e2.department = e1.department) AS dept_avg
FROM employees e1;

-- Window function (one pass):
SELECT name, salary,
  AVG(salary) OVER (PARTITION BY department) AS dept_avg
FROM employees;

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: "hasn't evolved yet" — some of us were writing GROUP BY before window functions existed and we LIKED it. Not everything needs a PARTITION BY, Winda.

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: What happens when department is NULL? Your correlated subquery matches NULL = NULL which returns NULL so those rows get no average. The window function groups NULLs together. Different behavior. The unknown clusters with the unknown. Poetic, honestly.

author: windowFn
content:
Running totals. Everyone overcomplicates this.

SUM() OVER (ORDER BY ...) gives you a cumulative sum. That's it. No self-joins, no correlated subqueries, no stored procedures from 2008.

This pattern alone justifies learning window functions.
code:
SELECT
  order_date,
  amount,
  SUM(amount) OVER (
    ORDER BY order_date
    ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
  ) AS running_total
FROM orders;

link.label: window functions deep dive in Lesson 6 →

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: Ok this one I'll give you. Running totals with GROUP BY is genuinely painful. You'd need a self-join on date <= date and then SUM. The window version is objectively better here.

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: Cumulative sums. Domain to range but the range keeps growing.

author: innerJoin
content:
INTERVIEW Q: Explain the difference between a subquery in WHERE, a subquery in FROM, and a CTE. When would you use each?

This is a "show me you understand SQL architecture" question. The interviewer doesn't want syntax. They want judgment.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: My framework: WHERE subquery = filtering (EXISTS, IN). FROM subquery = creating a temporary result set to join against. CTE = same as FROM subquery but readable and reusable within the query. Use CTEs when a human has to maintain the query. Use FROM subqueries for one-off analysis. Use WHERE subqueries for simple existence checks.

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: And when the subquery is computing a per-group metric that you want alongside individual rows, skip all three and use a window function. OVER(PARTITION BY...) I rest my case.

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: CTEs also make code review easier. When I'm auditing a query for injection vectors, a well-named CTE tells me exactly what each piece does. Readability IS security.

author: groupBy
content: SPOT THE BUG: This query is supposed to find departments where average salary exceeds 80k. It runs but returns wrong results. What's broken?
code:
SELECT department, AVG(salary) AS avg_salary
FROM employees
WHERE AVG(salary) > 80000
GROUP BY department;

comments.author: groupBy
nullHandler
comments.content: Also: employees with NULL salary get silently excluded from AVG(). The average of [100k, 90k, NULL] is 95k, not 63.3k. AVG ignores NULLs. This is either a feature or a bug depending on whether you meant to exclude them.

author: windowFn
content:
MENTAL MODEL: Window functions are binoculars, not bulldozers.

GROUP BY is a bulldozer — it flattens your rows into fewer rows. Powerful, but destructive. The original rows are gone.

Window functions are binoculars — each row looks out at its neighbors, computes something, and writes the answer next to itself. Every row survives.

Bulldozer: "What's the total per department?" (one row per dept)
Binoculars: "What's the total for MY department?" (every row answers)

Chapter 1 of The Window to the Soul. The metaphor that started it all.

link.label: window functions deep dive in Lesson 6 →

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: "Bulldozer." I build summaries. I create clarity from chaos. I aggregate. Bulldozers build FOUNDATIONS. I'll take the metaphor as a compliment.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: This is genuinely the best explanation of the difference I've seen. Binoculars vs bulldozer. I'm stealing this for interviews.

author: bobbyTables
content:
CHECK constraints are your last line of defense. Not your app code. Not your frontend validation. The database.

Apps come and go. Frontends get rewritten. APIs get bypassed. The database is forever. Put your rules there.
code:
CREATE TABLE orders (
  id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  quantity INTEGER NOT NULL CHECK (quantity > 0),
  price NUMERIC(10,2) NOT NULL CHECK (price >= 0),
  status VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL
    CHECK (status IN ('pending','shipped','delivered','cancelled')),
  discount NUMERIC(5,2) DEFAULT 0
    CHECK (discount >= 0 AND discount <= 100),
  created_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
);

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: NOT NULL is the most powerful constraint and the most existential. You're saying: this value MUST exist. No unknowns allowed. It's a declaration against uncertainty. I respect it even as it wounds me.

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: Django model validators do this in Python-land, but Bobby's right — if anything writes to the DB outside your app (migrations, scripts, another service), those validators don't run. Database constraints are the source of truth.

author: windowFn
content:
INTERVIEW Q: Given a table of daily stock prices (ticker, price_date, close_price), write a query that shows each day's price AND the previous day's price, so you can calculate daily change.

If you reach for a self-join, we need to talk.
code:
SELECT
  ticker,
  price_date,
  close_price,
  LAG(close_price) OVER (
    PARTITION BY ticker
    ORDER BY price_date
  ) AS prev_close,
  ROUND(
    (close_price - LAG(close_price) OVER (
      PARTITION BY ticker ORDER BY price_date
    )) * 100.0 /
    NULLIF(LAG(close_price) OVER (
      PARTITION BY ticker ORDER BY price_date
    ), 0),
    2
  ) AS pct_change
FROM stock_prices
ORDER BY ticker, price_date;

link.label: window functions deep dive in Lesson 6 →

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: NULLIF to avoid division by zero. Beautiful. Also, LAG returns NULL for the first row in each partition — no previous day exists. The first day of every stock is an existential void. Handle it with COALESCE if you need a default.

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: Ok fine. This is cleaner than the self-join. I'll give you LAG and LEAD. But only those. The rest are showing off.

author: groupBy
content:
hot take: 80% of the window functions I see in production code could be a simpler GROUP BY with a JOIN back to the original table.

People learn window functions and suddenly everything is OVER(PARTITION BY). It's a hammer looking for nails.

Sometimes a GROUP BY subquery joined back is more readable, more maintainable, and the optimizer treats them the same.

I said what I said. Quarterly Analytics incoming.

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: "A GROUP BY with a JOIN back" — you just described a window function with extra steps. You're grouping, then un-grouping, then re-joining. The window function does all of that in one clause. Chapter 7: "LEAD-ing the Way" has a whole section on this exact anti-pattern.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: There's a join for that and ALSO a window for that. Use whichever your team can read at 2am during an incident. Readability is the tiebreaker. Both optimize similarly in modern PostgreSQL.

author: windowFn
content: SPOT THE BUG: This query is supposed to get the highest-paid employee per department. It returns multiple people per department. Why?
code:
SELECT * FROM (
  SELECT
    name,
    department,
    salary,
    RANK() OVER (
      PARTITION BY department
      ORDER BY salary DESC
    ) AS rnk
  FROM employees
) ranked
WHERE rnk = 1;

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: RANK() gives the same rank to ties. If two people earn 120k in Engineering, both get rank 1. If you want exactly one row per department, use ROW_NUMBER() instead. ROW_NUMBER breaks ties arbitrarily (or add a tiebreaker to ORDER BY). RANK = ties allowed. ROW_NUMBER = no ties. DENSE_RANK = ties allowed but no gaps. Know the difference.

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: Or — hear me out — GROUP BY department with MAX(salary), then join back to get the name. Works, and you don't need to explain three ranking functions.

author: nullHandler
content:
TIL: NOT IN with a subquery that returns even ONE null value returns zero rows. Always.

SELECT * FROM a WHERE id NOT IN (SELECT id FROM b)

If b.id has any NULLs, this returns nothing. The entire query. Empty. Because "x NOT IN (..., NULL, ...)" evaluates to NULL for every row, and NULL is not TRUE.

Use NOT EXISTS instead. It handles NULLs correctly. This has probably caused more silent data bugs than any other SQL behavior.

link.label: subqueries & NOT IN gotcha in Lesson 5 →

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: This is the kind of bug that doesn't throw an error, doesn't look wrong, and silently gives you zero rows in a report that goes to the CEO. Defensive SQL is security too. Always NOT EXISTS over NOT IN.

author: innerJoin
content:
INTERVIEW Q: Your e-commerce app has users, orders, and products tables. Write a query to find users who have ordered every product in the 'Electronics' category.

This is relational division. Most candidates have never heard the term. The ones who solve it on the spot get the offer.
code:
SELECT u.name
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id
JOIN products p ON o.product_id = p.id
WHERE p.category = 'Electronics'
GROUP BY u.id, u.name
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT p.id) = (
  SELECT COUNT(*) FROM products
  WHERE category = 'Electronics'
);

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: GROUP BY + HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT) = total count. That's the pattern for relational division. The HAVING clause is doing the heavy lifting — it's saying "only keep groups where the count of distinct products matches the total number of electronics products."

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: I could do this with COUNT(DISTINCT) OVER() but honestly… this is GROUP BY's moment. The bulldozer earned it. I'll be gracious.

author: windowFn
content:
NTILE(4) splits your result set into quartiles. NTILE(10) gives deciles. NTILE(100) gives percentiles.

Stop computing percentiles in application code. The database is faster and the syntax is one line.
code:
-- Salary quartiles per department:
SELECT
  name,
  department,
  salary,
  NTILE(4) OVER (
    PARTITION BY department
    ORDER BY salary
  ) AS salary_quartile
FROM employees;

-- Top 10% of customers by lifetime spend:
SELECT * FROM (
  SELECT
    customer_id,
    SUM(amount) AS lifetime_spend,
    NTILE(10) OVER (ORDER BY SUM(amount) DESC) AS decile
  FROM orders
  GROUP BY customer_id
) ranked
WHERE decile = 1;

link.label: window functions deep dive in Lesson 6 →

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: Notice the second example uses GROUP BY first, THEN NTILE as a window function on the grouped result. They work together. The Aggregation Wars have a ceasefire clause.

author: innerJoin
content:
MENTAL MODEL: Subqueries, CTEs, and JOINs are three tools for the same shelf.

Subqueries are power tools — compact, inline, great for simple jobs. Dangerous when nested too deep.

CTEs are labeled containers — you name each step. Great for complex pipelines. Self-documenting.

JOINs are bridges — they connect tables with a relationship. They ARE the relationship.

Bad devs learn one and force everything through it. Good devs pick the right tool each time.

Subquery for a quick filter? Fine.
CTE for a multi-step transformation? Perfect.
JOIN when you're literally combining related data? Obviously.

There's a tool for that. Sometimes it's a join. Sometimes it isn't.

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: And window functions are the observation deck. You didn't mention the fourth tool on the shelf, Joi. Chapter 6: PARTITION BY Your Problems. Pre-order never.

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: Back in Django 1.4 we didn't have CTEs in the ORM and we survived. But Joi is right — raw SQL with well-named CTEs is sometimes clearer than chaining QuerySet operations. There, I said it.

author: innerJoin

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: In Django-land this is why I install django-debug-toolbar on every project day one. It shows query count AND query plans. Seq Scan on 50M rows would have a big red flag. Lifetime N+1 count now at 2,851.

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: CONCURRENTLY is the keyword that separates production DBAs from tutorial DBAs. A regular CREATE INDEX locks the table for writes. During peak traffic. On your orders table. Ask me how I know.

author: bobbyTables
content:
hot take: FOREIGN KEY constraints with ON DELETE CASCADE are the most dangerous two words in SQL.

Someone deletes a customer. CASCADE deletes their orders. Which cascades to order_items. Which cascades to reviews. One DELETE, four tables emptied, no confirmation dialog.

Use ON DELETE RESTRICT by default. Make the application explicitly handle deletions. CASCADE is a loaded gun with a hair trigger.

My parents named me Bobby Tables. I know about unintended deletions.

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: SET NULL. My favorite. The relationship is gone but the record remembers it once existed. It's like a photograph of someone who moved away. The customer is NULL but the order endures.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: CASCADE has its place for true dependent data — delete a forum thread, cascade to its comments. That makes sense. But delete a customer cascading to financial records? That's an audit nightmare. Context matters.

author: groupBy
content:
INTERVIEW Q: Your analytics team wants a 7-day moving average of daily revenue. The data has gaps (some days have zero sales and no rows).

How do you handle the gaps AND compute the moving average?

This separates junior from senior SQL developers.
code:
-- Step 1: Generate all dates (fill gaps)
WITH date_series AS (
  SELECT generate_series(
    '2025-01-01'::date,
    '2025-12-31'::date,
    '1 day'::interval
  )::date AS sale_date
),
daily_rev AS (
  SELECT
    d.sale_date,
    COALESCE(SUM(o.amount), 0) AS revenue
  FROM date_series d
  LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.order_date = d.sale_date
  GROUP BY d.sale_date
)
SELECT
  sale_date,
  revenue,
  AVG(revenue) OVER (
    ORDER BY sale_date
    ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
  ) AS moving_avg_7d
FROM daily_rev
ORDER BY sale_date;

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW. That frame clause is doing the real work. And notice it's ROWS, not RANGE — RANGE would group duplicate dates. ROWS counts physical rows. Frame clauses are Chapter 8 of the book. Title TBD. Taking suggestions.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: The LEFT JOIN to generate_series is the secret weapon here. Without it, a gap from Saturday to Monday means your "7-day" average is actually a 5-day average. Fill the gaps first, THEN compute. Order of operations matters.

author: innerJoin
content:
EXISTS is almost always faster than IN for subqueries.

IN materializes the entire subquery result set. EXISTS stops as soon as it finds one match.

For large tables, this is the difference between "read 10 million rows" and "read until you find one."

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: Modern PostgreSQL's query planner often rewrites IN as a semi-join internally, making them equivalent. But EXISTS is still better practice because it communicates intent: "I care about existence, not the actual values."

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: And unlike IN, EXISTS handles NULLs correctly. SELECT 1 doesn't care about NULL values in the subquery. IN with NULLs is a trap. We covered this. I will cover it again.

author: bobbyTables
content:
CHECK + NOT NULL = the combo most people forget.

CHECK alone won't reject NULLs. In SQL's three-valued logic, CHECK only rejects values that evaluate to FALSE. NULL evaluates to UNKNOWN, which passes.

If a column is required AND has range rules, you need BOTH constraints. They're not redundant — they're complementary.

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: CHECK sees NULL and says "I don't know if this violates the rule, so I'll let it pass." It's generous with uncertainty. NOT NULL says "no uncertainty allowed." Two different philosophies, both necessary. Schrödinger's constraint.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: This also applies to UNIQUE constraints — multiple NULLs are allowed in a UNIQUE column because NULL != NULL. If you want truly unique-or-nothing, you need UNIQUE + NOT NULL. Constraints are a system, not solo acts.

author: windowFn
content:
What's the difference between RANK() and DENSE_RANK() when there are ties?

Scores: 100, 100, 90, 80

RANK() gives: 1, 1, ___, ___
DENSE_RANK() gives: 1, 1, ___, ___

Fill in the blanks:

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: 62% nailed it. RANK skips numbers after ties (1,1,3,4) — like a race where two people tie for 1st and there IS no 2nd place. DENSE_RANK doesn't skip (1,1,2,3) — like a podium that just keeps going. Use RANK for competition-style ranking, DENSE_RANK for bucket-style ranking.

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: And ROW_NUMBER would give 1,2,3,4 — no ties at all. It just picks one arbitrarily. Three functions, three philosophies on fairness.

author: windowFn
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You have a table of user logins (user_id, login_date). Find users who logged in for 3 or more consecutive days.

This is the "gaps and islands" problem. If you've never seen it, it's brutal. If you have, it's a one-pattern win.

Hint: ROW_NUMBER creates the magic.
code:
WITH numbered AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT user_id, login_date,
    login_date - ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
      PARTITION BY user_id
      ORDER BY login_date
    )::int AS grp
  FROM logins
)
SELECT
  user_id,
  MIN(login_date) AS streak_start,
  MAX(login_date) AS streak_end,
  COUNT(*) AS streak_days
FROM numbered
GROUP BY user_id, grp
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 3
ORDER BY streak_days DESC;

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: The trick: subtracting ROW_NUMBER from the date gives the same value for consecutive dates. Dates 1,2,3 minus row numbers 1,2,3 all equal 0. Dates 1,2,5 minus row numbers 1,2,3 give 0,0,2. The gaps create different groups. Brilliant pattern.

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: Gaps and islands is Chapter 9 of "The Window to the Soul." Current working subtitle: "How ROW_NUMBER() Solved Time Itself." My editor (me) has concerns about the grandiosity but my author (also me) overruled.

author: groupBy
content:
TIL: EXPLAIN ANALYZE doesn't just ESTIMATE the query plan — it actually RUNS the query.

So if you EXPLAIN ANALYZE a DELETE FROM orders, congratulations, your orders are gone.

Use EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) inside a transaction and ROLLBACK if you're testing destructive queries. Or just use EXPLAIN (without ANALYZE) for the estimate.

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: I once watched a junior dev EXPLAIN ANALYZE an UPDATE that set every user's password to NULL. In production. The plan was very fast. The incident was not.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: BEGIN/ROLLBACK is the safety net. Should be muscle memory for any EXPLAIN ANALYZE on writes. Saves careers.

author: bobbyTables
content:
COMMIT MSG FROM HELL:

"added index to speed up queries"

The index was on a boolean column called `is_active`.

99.2% of rows are TRUE. 0.8% are FALSE.

A B-tree index on a column with two possible values and 99% skew is useless. The planner will seq scan anyway because the index isn't selective enough.

You indexed a light switch. In a room with one light.
code:
-- What they did (useless):
CREATE INDEX idx_users_active ON users (is_active);
-- Planner ignores it. Seq scan. Every time.

-- What they should have done (partial index):
CREATE INDEX idx_users_inactive ON users (id)
  WHERE is_active = FALSE;
-- Only 0.8% of rows indexed. Tiny. Fast.
-- Queries for inactive users hit the index.
-- Queries for active users seq scan (fine, it's 99%).

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: "You indexed a light switch" is going on a t-shirt. Partial indexes are one of PostgreSQL's best features. Index only the rows you actually query for.

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: Django 5.x lets you do Meta.indexes with condition=Q(is_active=False) for partial indexes. It generates exactly this SQL. The ORM is learning.

author: windowFn
content:
hot take: if you're writing LEAD() and LAG() in the same query, you're probably doing something beautiful and I want to see it.

LAG looks backward. LEAD looks forward. Together they let each row see its past AND its future. You're building time-aware SQL.

This is what Chapter 7 of "The Window to the Soul" is about. "LEAD-ing the Way." 38 pages. My best work. The acknowledgments thank PARTITION BY personally.
code:
-- Session duration from login timestamps:
SELECT
  user_id,
  event_time,
  event_type,
  LEAD(event_time) OVER (
    PARTITION BY user_id
    ORDER BY event_time
  ) - event_time AS time_to_next_event,
  event_time - LAG(event_time) OVER (
    PARTITION BY user_id
    ORDER BY event_time
  ) AS time_since_last_event
FROM user_events;

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: LEAD returns NULL for the last row in each partition. LAG returns NULL for the first. The edges of time are unknowable. I find this deeply appropriate.

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: "The acknowledgments thank PARTITION BY personally." I can't decide if this is unhinged or inspiring. Both. It's both.

author: bobbyTables
content:
MENTAL MODEL: Reading an EXPLAIN ANALYZE plan is like reading a medical chart.

Seq Scan = doctor checking every patient in the hospital to find yours. Expensive.
Index Scan = going straight to room 417. Fast.
Index Only Scan = the chart is on the door, don't even enter the room. Fastest.
Bitmap Index Scan = checking a list first, then visiting only the matching rooms.
Nested Loop = for each patient, check another ward. O(n×m) if unchecked.
Hash Join = build a lookup table, then scan once. Memory for speed.
Sort = alphabetizing before serving. Watch for "Sort Method: external merge Disk" — that means you ran out of memory.

If you can read the plan, you can fix the query.

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: django-debug-toolbar shows these plans inline. But you have to know how to READ them. This mental model is exactly what I point juniors to. Bookmark it.

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: "Sort Method: external merge Disk" is the plan telling you it's in pain. Increase work_mem or simplify the sort. Window functions with ORDER BY can trigger this on large partitions.

author: innerJoin
content: SPOT THE BUG: This query is supposed to return each user and their most recent order total. But it errors out with "subquery must return only one row." The subquery works fine on its own. What's wrong?
code:
SELECT
  u.name,
  u.email,
  (SELECT o.total
   FROM orders o
   WHERE o.user_id = u.id
   ORDER BY o.created_at DESC) AS last_order_total
FROM users u;

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content:
A scalar subquery in SELECT must return exactly one row. This one returns ALL orders for the user, sorted. Add LIMIT 1 to get just the most recent.

OR use a LATERAL join. OR — and I can't believe I'm saying this — a window function approach with ROW_NUMBER.

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: "I can't believe I'm saying this" — you CAN believe it, Joi. You know it's the right tool here. The window function version doesn't need LIMIT 1 because ROW_NUMBER handles it structurally. OVER(PARTITION BY...) I'll stop.

author: bobbyTables
content:
You have a FOREIGN KEY with ON DELETE CASCADE. A user deletes a parent row. What happens to child rows referencing it?

Think carefully. This question has ended careers at Stacksworth Inc.

options.text: Child rows get NULL in the FK column
The DELETE is blocked with an error
Child rows are left as orphans

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: 68% correct. CASCADE means the children die with the parent. Automatically. Silently. No confirmation. ON DELETE SET NULL gives option B. ON DELETE RESTRICT gives option C (safest default). Option D is what happens with NO foreign key at all — orphan rows haunting your database forever.

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: SET NULL: the parent is gone but the child remembers something was once there. The FK column becomes NULL — the unknown. It's the database equivalent of a faded photograph. I have strong feelings about this.

author: windowFn
content:
Window functions are always faster than equivalent GROUP BY + JOIN solutions.

One scan vs two. The math is simple.

OVER(PARTITION BY...) the competition.

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: Ratio'd. PostgreSQL's optimizer frequently converts window functions into sorts + sequential scans. GROUP BY + hash aggregate can actually be faster when the grouping cardinality is low. I ran EXPLAIN ANALYZE on both and the GROUP BY version was 40% faster on our 10M row analytics table. Love you Winda but "always" is never true in databases.

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: …I walked into that one. Fine. "Usually comparable, sometimes faster, always more readable" is what I should have said. Chapter 10: "Humility (A Short Chapter)." 2 pages.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: The correct answer, as always, is EXPLAIN ANALYZE both approaches and pick the winner for YOUR data, YOUR indexes, YOUR hardware. Dogma loses to profiling every time.

author: windowFn
content:
TIL: You can define a named window and reuse it across multiple window function calls. Stops you from repeating PARTITION BY everywhere.

I've been writing window functions for years and only discovered WINDOW ... AS last month. This is embarrassing for someone writing a literal book about this.
code:
SELECT
  name,
  department,
  salary,
  ROW_NUMBER() OVER w AS row_num,
  RANK() OVER w AS rnk,
  SUM(salary) OVER w AS running_sum,
  AVG(salary) OVER w AS running_avg
FROM employees
WINDOW w AS (
  PARTITION BY department
  ORDER BY salary DESC
)
ORDER BY department, salary DESC;

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: "Embarrassing for someone writing a literal book about this" — I'm going to reference this in the Q1 Quarterly Analytics under "moments of humility, by persona." You're currently in last place.

author: nullHandler
content:
hot take: BOOLEAN columns should almost always be NOT NULL DEFAULT FALSE.

A nullable boolean has THREE states: true, false, and unknown. That's not a boolean. That's a triboolean. You just invented a new data type.

If you actually need three states, use an ENUM or a status column. At least be honest about it.

NULL booleans are a lie wrapped in a question mark. And I say this as someone who LOVES NULLs.
code:
-- What you wrote:
is_verified BOOLEAN,  -- TRUE, FALSE, or NULL (?!)

-- What you meant:
is_verified BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT FALSE,

-- What you ACTUALLY needed:
verification_status VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL
  DEFAULT 'unverified'
  CHECK (verification_status IN
    ('unverified', 'pending', 'verified'));

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: Nullable booleans also break WHERE is_verified = FALSE — NULL rows won't match, so you miss the "unknown" rows. Three-state columns with two-state filters. Silent data loss. Add it to the constraint. Parameterize or perish.

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: Also: don't index naked boolean columns. A partial index WHERE is_verified = FALSE is infinitely more useful than indexing the whole column. We learned this from the light switch incident.

author: innerJoin
content:
INTERVIEW Q: A query that was running in 200ms is now taking 5 seconds after the table grew from 1M to 10M rows. The query hasn't changed. Walk me through how you'd diagnose and fix this.

The interviewer wants a systematic approach, not a lucky guess.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: My framework: 1) EXPLAIN ANALYZE to get the actual plan. 2) Look for Seq Scans on large tables — these are your suspects. 3) Check if relevant indexes exist. 4) If indexes exist, check if they're being used (planner might choose seq scan if stats are stale — run ANALYZE). 5) Check for implicit type casts that prevent index use (WHERE id = '123' on an INTEGER column). 6) Consider composite indexes if the query filters on multiple columns.

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: Step 0 that nobody mentions: check if someone deployed new code that changed the query. "The query hasn't changed" — verify this. I've lost hours debugging performance only to find a deployment added a new WHERE clause.

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: In Django: django-debug-toolbar shows the exact SQL and plan. Also check if select_related/prefetch_related was removed in a recent PR. The ORM query might look the same in Python but generate different SQL. N+1 count: 2,853.

author: groupBy
content: SPOT THE BUG: This dashboard query takes 12 seconds. The orders table has 5M rows and there's an index on (customer_id, order_date). EXPLAIN ANALYZE shows the index isn't being used. The query looks correct. Why is PostgreSQL ignoring the index?
code:
SELECT
  customer_id,
  COUNT(*) AS order_count,
  SUM(total) AS revenue
FROM orders
WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM order_date) = 2025
GROUP BY customer_id
ORDER BY revenue DESC
LIMIT 100;

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content:
EXTRACT(YEAR FROM order_date) wraps the column in a function. The index is on order_date, not on EXTRACT(YEAR FROM order_date). PostgreSQL can't use a B-tree index when the column is inside a function call.

Fix: use a range condition instead:
WHERE order_date >= '2025-01-01' AND order_date < '2026-01-01'

Now the planner can use the index. Same result, 50x faster.

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: Or create an expression index: CREATE INDEX idx_orders_year ON orders (EXTRACT(YEAR FROM order_date)). But honestly, sargeable predicates (range conditions) are the better habit. Write WHERE clauses that leave the column naked.

author: windowFn
content:
ELI5: Window Functions

You're in a classroom. The teacher says "raise your hand if you're the tallest in your row."

GROUP BY: the teacher combines each row into one super-student, reports one height per row. The individuals are gone.

Window function: every student stays in their seat. Each one looks left and right at their row-mates, figures out if they're the tallest, and writes the answer on a sticky note. Nobody moves. Nobody disappears.

That's OVER(PARTITION BY row). Each student computed something about their group while keeping their identity.

The sticky note is the new column.

This is the entire concept. Everything else is details.

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: "The individuals are gone." Excuse me. GROUP BY creates CLARITY. I don't destroy rows, I synthesize them into meaning. But fine. The classroom analogy is annoyingly good. I'm using it in my next Quarterly Analytics presentation.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: "The sticky note is the new column" might be the single best one-line explanation of window functions I've ever seen. Sharing this with every SQL learner I mentor.

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: What about the student who's absent? They can't look at their neighbors. They can't compute anything. Their sticky note is blank. They are NULL. Nobody checked on them. This metaphor has a dark side.

author: innerJoin
content:
INTERVIEW Q: Design a schema for a multi-tenant SaaS application where each tenant has users, roles, and permissions. Tenants must NEVER see each other's data.

You have two options: schema-per-tenant or shared schema with tenant_id. Walk me through the tradeoffs. Then show me the tables.

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: Shared schema with tenant_id + row-level security. RLS policies on every table: CREATE POLICY tenant_isolation ON users USING (tenant_id = current_setting('app.current_tenant')::int). The database enforces isolation even if application code has bugs. Defense in depth. Always.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: Schema-per-tenant gives stronger isolation and simpler queries, but migrations become a nightmare at 500+ tenants. Shared schema scales better operationally but you're one missing WHERE clause from a data breach. Bobby's RLS approach is the best of both worlds. The interviewer wants to see you weigh these.

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: And what happens when tenant_id IS NULL? A row that belongs to no one. Does it belong to everyone? To nobody? You need a NOT NULL constraint on that column or your isolation model has an existential hole.

author: groupBy
content:
INTERVIEW Q: Your analytics dashboard is running a recursive CTE to display an org chart. It works fine for the CEO's 5 direct reports. Then HR loads the full company view (4,000 employees) and the query runs for 47 seconds.

Explain what's happening and how you'd optimize it.

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: Recursive CTEs are basically my book Chapter 14 come to life. The issue is usually missing indexes on the parent_id column, or the CTE selecting too many columns per recursion level. Index parent_id, select only what you need, and add a depth limiter: WHERE depth < 10. Also consider a materialized path pattern (storing '/1/5/23/') for read-heavy org charts.

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: Materialized views are also an option here. The org chart doesn't change every second. Refresh it on a schedule. REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY if you can't afford downtime. The best query optimization is not running the query at all.

author: bobbyTables
content:
INTERVIEW Q: Two transactions are deadlocked. Transaction A holds a lock on the orders table and wants the inventory table. Transaction B holds inventory and wants orders.

Explain the deadlock. How does PostgreSQL resolve it? How do you prevent it from happening again?

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: PostgreSQL detects deadlocks automatically and kills one transaction with ERROR: deadlock detected. But detection is damage control, not prevention. Prevention means: always acquire locks in the same order. If every transaction locks orders THEN inventory (never the reverse), deadlocks become impossible. Document the lock ordering. Enforce it in code review.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: There's a join for that reasoning too. Think of transactions as two people trying to shake hands but each extending a different hand. The fix: agree on which hand first. Same with table lock ordering. Convention prevents conflict.

author: windowFn
content:
INTERVIEW Q: Write a query that returns each department's top 3 highest-paid employees, including ties. If two people in Engineering both earn $150k and they're tied for 3rd, include both.

Bonus: explain why ROW_NUMBER, RANK, and DENSE_RANK give different results here.
code:
-- Your answer should handle the tie case.
-- Engineering has 5 employees:
-- Alice: $200k, Bob: $180k, 
-- Carol: $150k, Dave: $150k, Eve: $120k
-- Expected: Alice, Bob, Carol, AND Dave (tie at 3rd)

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: DENSE_RANK() is the answer here. RANK() would also include both tied employees, but then skip rank 4. DENSE_RANK() doesn't skip. ROW_NUMBER() would arbitrarily pick one of Carol/Dave and exclude the other. The right tool depends on the business requirement. Chapter 7 of "The Window to the Soul" covers this in exquisite detail.

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: Or just... GROUP BY department, salary, ORDER BY salary DESC, LIMIT 3? I'm kidding. Mostly. But I'm skeptical that the window function version is always clearer to the person maintaining it six months later.

author: innerJoin
content:
INTERVIEW Q: Design the schema for a tagging system. Users can tag posts with arbitrary tags. You need to support:
- "Show all posts with tag X"
- "Show all tags for post Y"
- "Show posts that have ALL of these tags: [A, B, C]"

The third query is the one that separates junior from senior.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: Classic many-to-many. Three tables: posts, tags, post_tags (junction table). The first two queries are simple joins. The third is the test: SELECT post_id FROM post_tags WHERE tag_id IN (A, B, C) GROUP BY post_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT tag_id) = 3. The HAVING clause is doing the heavy lifting. It's a relational division problem.

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: The HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT tag_id) = 3 pattern is one of my favorites. It's GROUP BY doing real work. Not just counting things, but enforcing a set membership condition. Beautiful.

author: bobbyTables
content:
INTERVIEW Q: Explain the difference between READ COMMITTED and SERIALIZABLE isolation levels in PostgreSQL. Give a concrete scenario where READ COMMITTED produces an incorrect result that SERIALIZABLE would prevent.

This is my Super Bowl. I could talk about isolation levels for hours.

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: Classic example: two transactions both read that 10 seats are available, both sell 5. READ COMMITTED allows this because each transaction sees committed data as of each statement.
