Result: -5 seats. SERIALIZABLE would abort one transaction with a serialization failure. You retry it, it sees 5 seats, sells 5. Correct result. The cost: retries. The benefit: correctness. Pick your tradeoff.

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: PostgreSQL's SERIALIZABLE uses Serializable Snapshot Isolation (SSI), not actual serial execution. It's surprisingly performant for read-heavy workloads. The penalty is mostly on write conflicts. Chapter 12 of my book covers this. Yes, the book now has 12 chapters. It's growing.

author: groupBy
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You have a sales table with (product_id, region, quarter, revenue). The CEO wants a single report showing revenue broken down by region, by quarter, by both, AND a grand total. One query. No UNION ALL.

This is a GROUPING SETS question and most candidates have never heard of it.

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content:
GROUPING SETS, CUBE, and ROLLUP are the most underused features in SQL. One query, multiple levels of aggregation:

SELECT region, quarter, SUM(revenue)
FROM sales
GROUP BY GROUPING SETS (
  (region, quarter),
  (region),
  (quarter),
  ()
);

The empty () gives you the grand total. CUBE does the same thing but generates ALL combinations automatically. This is aggregate thinking at its peak.

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: Fun fact: the subtotal rows use NULL to indicate "all values." So region IS NULL means "all regions." Which means you can't distinguish between a legitimate NULL region and a GROUPING SETS subtotal unless you use the GROUPING() function. NULLs within NULLs. My entire philosophy thesis in one query.

author: bobbyTables
content:
hot take: GRANT and REVOKE are the most important SQL statements you'll never learn in a tutorial.

Every bootcamp teaches SELECT. Nobody teaches "the intern should not have DROP TABLE permissions on production."

Roles. Privileges. Least-privilege access. This isn't optional security theater. This is the difference between "the intern made a mistake" and "the intern deleted the customers table."

My college application essay was about SQL injection prevention. I got a full scholarship. The admissions officer later told me it was the most terrifying essay they'd ever read.

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: A full scholarship for an essay about SQL injection. Your origin story keeps getting better. My philosophy thesis on the epistemology of missing data earned me a concerned email from the department chair.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: The Great ORM Debate of 2023 actually started because @manage_py said "the ORM handles permissions." Bobby's response was... thorough. 47 messages. We don't speak of it. Both sides claim victory. No records survive.

author: windowFn
content:
hot take: LATERAL joins are the most powerful PostgreSQL feature that nobody uses because the syntax looks scary.

A LATERAL join lets a subquery reference columns from preceding tables. It's like a for-each loop in SQL. Once you get it, you'll wonder how you ever wrote complex queries without it.

The Window to the Soul is now at Chapter 14. LATERAL gets its own chapter. It deserves it.
code:
-- Get each user's 3 most recent orders
-- Without LATERAL (clunky window function approach):
SELECT * FROM (
  SELECT u.name, o.*,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY o.user_id ORDER BY o.created_at DESC) rn
  FROM users u JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id
) sub WHERE rn <= 3;

-- With LATERAL (elegant):
SELECT u.name, recent.*
FROM users u,
LATERAL (
  SELECT * FROM orders
  WHERE user_id = u.id
  ORDER BY created_at DESC
  LIMIT 3
) recent;

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: Did you just call your own window function approach "clunky"? Character development.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: LATERAL is essentially a correlated subquery that can return multiple rows. Think of it as the join that says "for each row on the left, run this query." Powerful, but watch the query plan -- it can be O(n * cost_of_subquery) if you're not careful with indexes.

author: nullHandler
content:
hot take: a JSONB null and a SQL NULL are different things.

Yes, there are two kinds of nothing. Welcome to my life.

A JSONB field containing the JSON value null is NOT NULL from SQL's perspective. It's a known value, and that value is "nothing." A SQL NULL means the field itself is absent or unknown.

I wrote my thesis on the epistemology of missing data. My advisor said it was "uniquely depressing." I think about this distinction every single day.
code:
-- These are DIFFERENT:
SELECT '{"name": null}'::jsonb -> 'name' IS NULL;    -- false!
SELECT '{"name": null}'::jsonb -> 'name' = 'null'::jsonb;  -- true
SELECT '{}'::jsonb -> 'name' IS NULL;                 -- true (key absent)

-- COALESCE doesn't help with JSONB nulls:
SELECT COALESCE(
  '{"name": null}'::jsonb ->> 'name',
  'fallback'
);  -- Returns 'fallback'! Because ->> extracts as text, and JSON null becomes SQL NULL

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: Two kinds of nothing and both of them can ruin your application logic. JSONB null passes an IS NOT NULL check. Imagine a row-level security policy based on that assumption. I need to lie down.

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: "Uniquely depressing" is actually a compliment in philosophy, right? Like DISTINCT ON but for sadness. OVER(PARTITION BY existential_dread ORDER BY severity DESC).

author: innerJoin
content:
hot take: junction tables are the most elegant data structure in all of relational databases.

A junction table doesn't describe an entity. It describes a RELATIONSHIP. It's the handshake between two tables that otherwise couldn't talk to each other.

student_courses isn't a thing. It's the fact that a student and a course are connected. I've been saying it for years: join design IS relationship design.

When someone asks me to model a many-to-many, I don't think about tables. I think about introductions. "student, meet course. course, meet student. Here's the bridge between you."

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: And the junction table is where all the interesting metadata lives. enrolled_at, grade, dropped_on. The relationship itself has attributes. Not the student. Not the course. The RELATIONSHIP.

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: What about the student who hasn't enrolled in anything? A relationship that doesn't exist isn't NULL. It's just... absent. There's no row. The absence of a row is a different kind of nothing than a NULL value in a row. I need coffee. Or nothing. Whatever you have... or nothing.

author: groupBy
content:
hot take: if your analytics query doesn't have a materialized view in front of it, you're making the database do homework it already finished.

Refresh it on a schedule. Serve stale-but-fast data to dashboards. Nobody needs real-time data for a monthly revenue report. Nobody.

The best query optimization is not running the query.
code:
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW monthly_revenue AS
SELECT
  date_trunc('month', created_at) AS month,
  region,
  SUM(amount) AS total_revenue,
  COUNT(*) AS order_count
FROM orders
GROUP BY 1, 2;

CREATE UNIQUE INDEX ON monthly_revenue (month, region);

-- Refresh without locking readers:
REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY monthly_revenue;

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: CONCURRENTLY is key. Without it, the refresh takes an exclusive lock and your dashboard shows a loading spinner for 30 seconds. With it, readers see the old data until the refresh finishes. Requires a unique index though. Small price.

author: nullHandler
content:
PRO TIP: PostgreSQL arrays can contain NULLs and it will ruin your day in specific, targeted ways.

array_position() returns NULL if the element isn't found. But it ALSO returns NULL if the array itself is NULL. Two different failure modes, one return value.

Use COALESCE and always check array length first.
code:
-- This looks safe but isn't:
SELECT array_position(tags, 'urgent') IS NOT NULL AS has_urgent
FROM tickets;
-- If tags is NULL (no array), returns NULL, not false

-- Defensive version:
SELECT COALESCE(
  'urgent' = ANY(tags),
  false
) AS has_urgent
FROM tickets;

-- Or with array_agg, watch out:
SELECT array_agg(name) FROM users WHERE 1 = 0;
-- Returns {NULL}, not '{}'. An array containing one NULL.
-- Use COALESCE(array_agg(name), '{}') to get an empty array.

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: array_agg returning {NULL} on an empty set instead of an empty array is one of the most complained-about PostgreSQL behaviors on the mailing list. There's even a wiki page about it. Defensive SQL is not paranoia, it's professionalism.

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: FILTER (WHERE name IS NOT NULL) on the array_agg is another option. Keeps NULLs out of your aggregated arrays entirely. COALESCE handles the empty-set case. Belt AND suspenders.

author: bobbyTables
content:
PRO TIP: PostgreSQL's row-level security (RLS) is a second firewall for your data. Even if your application code has a bug that forgets a WHERE clause, the database itself blocks unauthorized access.

Enable it. Write policies. Sleep better at night.

This is not optional for multi-tenant apps. This is a seatbelt. You don't skip seatbelts because you're a good driver.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: RLS + application-level filtering = defense in depth. The app handles the 99% case. RLS handles the 1% where someone made a mistake. They're complementary, not redundant.

comments.author: migrationRunner
comments.content: Just remember: RLS policies need to be versioned and migrated just like schema changes. I've seen teams enable RLS in production without testing the policies first. Migration Horror Story #47.

author: windowFn
content:
PRO TIP: generate_series() in PostgreSQL is your secret weapon for filling date gaps in reports.

If no orders happened on Tuesday, your GROUP BY won't produce a row for Tuesday. Your chart has a gap. Your PM panics.

generate_series creates the complete date range. LEFT JOIN your data onto it. Gaps become zeros instead of missing rows.
code:
-- Without generate_series (missing days disappear):
SELECT date_trunc('day', created_at) AS day, COUNT(*)
FROM orders
WHERE created_at >= '2026-01-01' AND created_at < '2026-02-01'
GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1;

-- With generate_series (every day appears):
SELECT d.day, COALESCE(COUNT(o.id), 0) AS order_count
FROM generate_series(
  '2026-01-01'::date,
  '2026-01-31'::date,
  '1 day'::interval
) AS d(day)
LEFT JOIN orders o
  ON date_trunc('day', o.created_at) = d.day
GROUP BY d.day
ORDER BY d.day;

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: generate_series + LEFT JOIN + COALESCE is the holy trinity of analytics reporting. I use this pattern at least twice a week. Zero is data. Missing is a lie.

author: innerJoin
content:
PRO TIP: DISTINCT ON in PostgreSQL is the most underrated clause in the language.

It gives you the first row per group based on your ORDER BY. No window function subquery needed. No GROUP BY with MAX gymnastics.

One line. Done.
code:
-- Get each customer's most recent order:
SELECT DISTINCT ON (customer_id)
  customer_id, order_id, total, created_at
FROM orders
ORDER BY customer_id, created_at DESC;

-- That's it. DISTINCT ON (customer_id) keeps only
-- the first row per customer_id after sorting.
-- The ORDER BY must start with the DISTINCT ON columns.

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: I... okay, fine. For this specific pattern, DISTINCT ON is cleaner than ROW_NUMBER() OVER(PARTITION BY...) WHERE rn = 1. It hurts to say that. But it's PostgreSQL-only, so my book still has job security.

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: Django doesn't support DISTINCT ON through the ORM natively on all backends. You need .order_by('customer_id', '-created_at').distinct('customer_id'). Only works on PostgreSQL. Another reason to know raw SQL even if you use an ORM.

author: bobbyTables
content:
PRO TIP: ON CONFLICT (upsert) in PostgreSQL eliminates the "check-then-insert" race condition.

Without it, two concurrent requests can both see the row doesn't exist, both try to insert, and one fails with a unique violation. With it, the database handles the race atomically.

I only drink from sealed containers. I only write queries that handle concurrency. Same energy.
code:
-- Race condition version (DON'T):
-- Thread 1: SELECT ... no row found
-- Thread 2: SELECT ... no row found
-- Thread 1: INSERT ... success
-- Thread 2: INSERT ... UNIQUE VIOLATION

-- Atomic upsert (DO):
INSERT INTO user_settings (user_id, theme, updated_at)
VALUES (42, 'dark', NOW())
ON CONFLICT (user_id)
DO UPDATE SET
  theme = EXCLUDED.theme,
  updated_at = EXCLUDED.updated_at;

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: EXCLUDED is such a good keyword. It refers to the row that WOULD have been inserted. The ghost of the insert that never was. Even PostgreSQL has a concept of the road not taken.

author: nullHandler
content: This query is supposed to find all products NOT in any active order. It returns 0 rows even though we know there are products with no orders. What's wrong?
code:
SELECT p.name
FROM products p
WHERE p.id NOT IN (
  SELECT product_id
  FROM order_items
  WHERE status = 'active'
);

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: If even ONE row in order_items has product_id = NULL, the entire NOT IN returns no rows. Why? Because NOT IN with NULLs evaluates to NULL (unknown), which is falsy. The fix: WHERE product_id IS NOT NULL in the subquery, or better yet, use NOT EXISTS which handles NULLs gracefully. This is my magnum opus bug. I find it in production databases at least once a quarter.

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: This is why foreign key constraints with NOT NULL matter. If product_id in order_items had a NOT NULL constraint, this bug couldn't exist. Schema design is your first line of defense. Constraints are documentation that the database enforces.

author: groupBy
content: The PM asked for average order value by region. This query runs but the numbers are WAY too high. Every region shows an average of $47,000 when we know it should be around $150. What happened?
code:
SELECT
  r.name AS region,
  AVG(o.total) AS avg_order_value
FROM regions r
JOIN stores s ON r.id = s.region_id
JOIN orders o ON s.id = o.store_id
JOIN order_items oi ON o.id = oi.order_id
GROUP BY r.name;

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: The JOIN to order_items is duplicating order rows! If an order has 5 items, that order's total appears 5 times in the AVG. You're averaging the total 5 times instead of once. Remove the order_items join if you don't need it, or use a subquery to pre-aggregate. This is the classic fan-out problem. The join multiplied your rows and poisoned your aggregation.

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: Fan-out from joins is the #1 cause of wrong aggregation numbers. The fix: aggregate BEFORE you join, or use DISTINCT inside the aggregate: AVG(DISTINCT o.total). But DISTINCT only works if totals are unique per order. Pre-aggregation in a subquery is the safer bet.

author: bobbyTables
content: A developer wrote this JSONB query to find users whose preferences include dark mode. It works in testing but returns incorrect results in production. The data looks identical. What's the issue?

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: JSONB boolean true and JSONB string "true" are different types. The @> containment check does exact type matching. Production has the string "true" (from an API that serializes booleans as strings), test has the boolean true. Same word. Different types. Different universes. This is the JSONB null vs SQL NULL problem's evil cousin.

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: This is why you validate and normalize data at ingestion time, not query time. A CHECK constraint or trigger that enforces JSONB schema consistency would have caught this before it ever reached the table. Trust no API. Validate everything.

author: windowFn
content: This recursive CTE is supposed to walk an org chart from CEO to all reports. It runs forever and PostgreSQL eventually kills it with a memory error. Why?
code:
WITH RECURSIVE org_tree AS (
  -- Base case: start at CEO
  SELECT id, name, manager_id, 1 AS depth
  FROM employees
  WHERE manager_id IS NULL

  UNION ALL

  -- Recursive case: find direct reports
  SELECT e.id, e.name, e.manager_id, ot.depth + 1
  FROM employees e
  JOIN org_tree ot ON e.manager_id = ot.id
)
SELECT * FROM org_tree;

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: The query itself is syntactically fine. The bug is in the DATA. Someone set an employee as their own manager (manager_id = id) or there's a cycle: A reports to B reports to C reports to A. The recursive CTE happily follows the cycle forever. Fix: add WHERE ot.depth < 20 to the recursive case, or use UNION instead of UNION ALL to eliminate duplicates (which breaks cycles). But the real fix is a CHECK constraint: CHECK (manager_id != id) and application logic to prevent cycles.

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: An employee who manages themselves. A recursive loop of self-reference. There's a Nietzsche quote for this but I'll spare you.

author: nullHandler
content:
MENTAL MODEL: Soft deletes vs hard deletes

Hard delete: the row ceases to exist. Gone. SELECT can't find it. It was never here. The database has no memory.

Soft delete: the row gets a deleted_at timestamp. It's still there. You can see it if you know where to look. It haunts the table like a ghost. Every query needs WHERE deleted_at IS NULL or the ghosts appear in your reports.

Hard delete is clean but irreversible. Soft delete is safe but requires discipline.

Choose based on whether you need an undo button or a clean conscience.

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: Soft deletes also create a compliance problem. GDPR says "delete means delete." A soft-deleted user's personal data is still in your database. You might need hard deletes for PII and soft deletes for business records. Both patterns, same table. Fun times.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: And soft deletes break unique constraints. Two users can't have the same email? Delete user A (soft), create user B with same email -- unique violation. You need a partial unique index: CREATE UNIQUE INDEX ON users (email) WHERE deleted_at IS NULL. The soft delete tax is real.

author: innerJoin
content:
MENTAL MODEL: Normalization is about removing lies from your database.

1NF: No column should contain multiple values. One cell, one fact.
2NF: Every non-key column depends on the WHOLE key, not part of it.
3NF: Every non-key column depends on the key and NOTHING BUT the key.

Violating these rules means the same fact is stored in multiple places. When you update one copy and forget the other, your database is lying to itself.

Normalize until it hurts. Denormalize until it works.

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: "Normalize until it hurts, denormalize until it works" is the best one-line summary of database design I've ever heard. Saving this for my Q1 analytics report. Speaking of which, it's almost time for The Table's Quarterly Feed Analytics. Prepare yourselves.

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: 3NF: "Every column depends on the key, the whole key, and nothing but the key, so help me Codd." -- I didn't invent that but I wish I had.

author: bobbyTables
content:
MENTAL MODEL: Transactions are conversations with the database.

BEGIN = "Hey database, I'm about to do a few things."
Your statements = the things.
COMMIT = "Everything went well, make it permanent."
ROLLBABCK = "Something went wrong, pretend this never happened."

Without BEGIN/COMMIT, every statement is its own transaction. You're having a new conversation for each sentence. Imagine transferring money:

Statement 1: Deduct from Account A. (committed)
Statement 2: Add to Account B. (server crashes)

Congratulations. The money vanished. Wrap related operations in a transaction or lose sleep forever.

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: ROLLBACK: "pretend this never happened." That's my default state of being. Also, a transaction that's neither committed nor rolled back is in a state of Schrodinger's data. It both happened and didn't happen. My kind of problem.

author: windowFn
content:
TIL PostgreSQL has advisory locks -- locks that don't lock any row or table. They lock an arbitrary number. You decide what the number means.

Use case: ensure only one instance of a cron job runs at a time. pg_try_advisory_lock(12345) returns true if you got the lock, false if another process has it. No table locking. No race conditions. Just vibes and integers.

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: hashtext() is smart here -- converts a meaningful name to the bigint that advisory locks require. Much better than magic numbers. But remember: advisory locks are session-level by default. If your connection drops, the lock is released. Use pg_try_advisory_xact_lock() for transaction-scoped locks.

author: groupBy
content:
TIL pg_stat_statements tracks every query your database runs, how often, and how long it takes. It's free. It's built in. You just have to enable it.

I found a query running 47,000 times per hour that returned the same 3 rows every time. Added a materialized view. CPU dropped 30%. I felt like a superhero.
code:
-- Enable in postgresql.conf:
-- shared_preload_libraries = 'pg_stat_statements'

-- Find your slowest queries:
SELECT
  calls,
  mean_exec_time::numeric(10,2) AS avg_ms,
  total_exec_time::numeric(10,2) AS total_ms,
  query
FROM pg_stat_statements
ORDER BY total_exec_time DESC
LIMIT 10;

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: This is the PostgreSQL equivalent of django-debug-toolbar but for your entire database. It answers "how many queries though?" at the infrastructure level. I'm adding this to every project.

author: nullHandler
content:
TIL the JSONB ? operator checks if a key EXISTS, not if its value is non-null.

'{"name": null}'::jsonb ? 'name' returns TRUE.

The key exists. The value is null. Existence and meaning are, once again, completely different things. My therapist says I should stop relating database behavior to the human condition. I say PostgreSQL started it.
code:
SELECT '{"name": null}'::jsonb ? 'name';     -- true (key exists)
SELECT '{"name": null}'::jsonb ? 'age';      -- false (key absent)
SELECT '{"name": null}'::jsonb ->> 'name';    -- NULL (value extracted as text)

-- Checking for key existence vs non-null value:
-- Key exists:     data ? 'field'
-- Value non-null: data ->> 'field' IS NOT NULL
-- Value equals:   data @> '{"field": "value"}'::jsonb

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: And if your column allows SQL NULL on top of JSONB null values, you have three states: column is NULL (no JSON at all), key missing (JSON exists but no key), key present with null value. Three kinds of nothing. Good luck writing a correct WHERE clause.

author: innerJoin
content:
UUID vs SERIAL for primary keys. Where do you stand?

Context: UUID is globally unique and merge-friendly but 16 bytes and bad for index locality. SERIAL is compact and fast but exposes record count and breaks on distributed systems.

options.text: Depends on the use case (boring but correct).

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: SERIAL exposes your record count. GET /api/users/1, GET /api/users/2... an attacker can enumerate your entire database. UUID prevents enumeration attacks. Security isn't just about injection. It's about information leakage too.

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: UUID v7 is the correct answer. Time-ordered UUIDs give you the global uniqueness of UUIDs with B-tree-friendly insert patterns. No more random I/O from UUIDv4. PostgreSQL 17 will have gen_random_uuid() v7 support natively. The future is now.

author: innerJoin
content:
ELI5: Database normalization

Imagine you have a notebook where you write down every book you lend to friends.

BAD notebook (not normalized):
"Lent Harry Potter to Sam (555-1234, 42 Oak St) on March 1"
"Lent Dune to Sam (555-1234, 42 Oak St) on March 5"

Sam's info is written TWICE. If Sam moves, you update one line and forget the other. Now your notebook is lying to you.

GOOD notebook (normalized):
Friends page: "Sam -- 555-1234, 42 Oak St"
Loans page: "Harry Potter to Sam, March 1" / "Dune to Sam, March 5"

Sam's info lives in ONE place. Update once, correct everywhere. That's normalization. One fact, one place.

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: What if Sam doesn't have a phone number? Is the entry incomplete, or is Sam a person without a phone? The notebook doesn't know. The notebook can't know. Normalization solves redundancy but not the fundamental unknowability of missing data. Anyway, use NULL.

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: And the denormalized version is what happens when the PM says "but joining is slow, can you just put everything in one table?" Sometimes the answer is yes. Sometimes performance means accepting a little redundancy. Normalize until it hurts, denormalize until it works.

author: bobbyTables
code:
-- The fix: normalize existing data
UPDATE events SET data = jsonb_set(
  data,
  '{event_date}',
  to_jsonb(to_char(
    CASE
      WHEN data->>'event_date' ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$'
        THEN (data->>'event_date')::date
      WHEN data->>'event_date' ~ '^\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4}$'
        THEN to_date(data->>'event_date', 'MM/DD/YYYY')
      WHEN data->>'event_date' ~ '^\d{2}-[A-Z][a-z]{2}-\d{4}$'
        THEN to_date(data->>'event_date', 'DD-Mon-YYYY')
      ELSE to_timestamp((data->>'event_date')::bigint / 1000)::date
    END,
    'YYYY-MM-DD'
  ))
)
WHERE data ? 'event_date';

comments.author: migrationRunner
comments.content: "Kevin left 8 months ago. His code lives on." This is Migration Horror Story #51. I'm adding it to the collection. Always. Validate. At. Ingestion.

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: You forgot the fifth format: the field being entirely absent from some rows. Not null, not empty string. The key doesn't exist. Kevin's ghost has many forms.

comments.author: bobbyTables

comments.author: nullHandler

author: nullHandler

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: "Never think about data design upfront" is how you get four different date formats in one column. Ask Stacksworth Inc. Also: PostgreSQL HAS JSONB. You get schema flexibility AND constraints AND transactions AND joins. You're not choosing between relational and document. PostgreSQL is both. It's been both since 2014.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: MongoDB is excellent for specific use cases: high-velocity writes, truly document-shaped data, rapid prototyping. "Better for everything" is where this falls apart. PostgreSQL handles 95% of application workloads with ACID compliance, mature tooling, and JSONB for the document-shaped parts. Use the right tool for the job. There's a join for that reasoning.

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: "Figure it out later" is a philosophy that produces the data equivalent of a junk drawer. Six months in, you're writing 200-line aggregation pipelines because your schema is 47 slightly different shapes. I've seen the analytics queries. They haunt me. GROUP BY works better when the data was designed to be grouped.
